name: Amateur hour
description: Early sound equipment allowed filmmakers and amateurs alike the ability to experiment often with affordable 16mm film.

key_work.title: Early sound recording equipment
key_work.brief_description: In 1935, the world’s first sound-on-film camera was released for the amateur market, the RCA Victor Sound 16mm camera. Like RCA’s Photophone system, it recorded sound directly onto the film’s edge via light reflected off a mirror, which was attached to a diaphragm that vibrated when the operator spoke.

constellations_primary.name: Amateur hour
constellations_primary.description: Early sound equipment allowed filmmakers and amateurs alike the ability to experiment often with affordable 16mm film.

start.title: PR-25 16mm Sound Cinecamera
start.brief_description: The RCA Victor Sound 16mm Camera was introduced in 1934, five years after Hollywood’s conversion to sound pictures. It was the world’s first 16mm sound on film camera and an early technical leap in the sound-on-film process for the amateur market.

constellations_other.name: Amateur hour
constellations_other.description: Early sound equipment allowed filmmakers and amateurs alike the ability to experiment often with affordable 16mm film.

end.title: Early Amateur Sound Film (1936–37)
end.brief_description: Amateur filmmaker Archie Stewart used an RCA Sound Camera to document family life from 1935. A member of the Amateur Cinema League, in 1937 he published one of the first articles on amateur sound, ‘A Talkie Movie Maker Speaks’, in the league’s magazine.

Like RCA’s Photophone system, it recorded sound directly onto the film’s edge via light reflected off a mirror, which was attached to a diaphragm that vibrated when the operator spoke.

links.description: The RCA Victor Sound 16mm Camera was introduced in 1934, five years after Hollywood’s conversion to sound pictures. The RCA Sound camera was the world’s first 16mm sound on film camera and was marketed towards amateur film makers.

start.title: Early Amateur Sound Film (1936–37)
start.brief_description: Amateur filmmaker Archie Stewart used an RCA Sound Camera to document family life from 1935. A member of the Amateur Cinema League, in 1937 he published one of the first articles on amateur sound, ‘A Talkie Movie Maker Speaks’, in the league’s magazine.

constellations_other.name: Amateur hour

end.title: Following
end.creator_credit: Christopher Nolan, Emma Thomas, Jeremy Theobald and Next Wave Films
end.headline_credit: United Kingdom and United States of America, 1999
end.brief_description: Bill, an idle, unemployed and aspiring writer, walks the crowded streets of London following randomly chosen strangers, a seemingly innocent activity that becomes dangerous when he crosses paths with a mysterious character…

constellations_other.name: Amateur hour
constellations_other.description: Early sound equipment allowed filmmakers and amateurs alike the ability to experiment often with affordable 16mm film.

end.creator_credit_for_label: Christopher Nolan, Emma Thomas, Jeremy Theobald and Next Wave Films
end.headline_credit_for_label: United Kingdom and United States of America, 1999

links.description: You wouldn’t consider him an amateur now, but director Christopher Nolan never studied film. His debut Following, was produced with almost amateur production values. Shot on black-and-white 16mm film that intensified the grittiness, scenes were heavily rehearsed to reduce takes.

end.title: Certain Women
end.creator_credit: Kelly Reichardt, April Napier, Anish Savjani, Neil Kopp, Vincent Savino, filmscience, Stage 6 Films, Anthony Gasparro and Maile Meloy

video_links.name: Certain Women - Official Trailer I HD I IFC Films

end.brief_description: Based on Maile Meloy’s short story collection, Both Ways Is the Only Way I Want It, Certain Women weaves in-and-out of the lives of a small-town lawyer, a woman building a country house, a night-school teacher and an isolated ranch hand.

constellations_other.name: Amateur hour
constellations_other.description: Early sound equipment allowed filmmakers and amateurs alike the ability to experiment often with affordable 16mm film.

end.creator_credit_for_label: Kelly Reichardt, April Napier, Anish Savjani, Neil Kopp, Vincent Savino, filmscience, Stage 6 Films, Anthony Gasparro and Maile Meloy

links.description: Kelly Reichardt also used 16mm to shoot Certain Women. Originally intending to shoot the film digitally, Reichardt and her DP Chris Blauvelt decided to use 16mm for Certain Women to capitalise on its warm grain and texture.

end.title: Dawson's Creek
end.creator_credit: Kevin Williamson, Paul Stupin, Tom Kapinos, Columbia TriStar Television, Granville Productions, Outerbanks Entertainment, Procter & Gamble Productions (PGP) and Sony Pictures Television Studios
end.brief_description: Dawson’s Creek is an American teen drama that portrays the fictional lives of a close-knit group of teenagers through high school and college.

constellations_other.name: Amateur hour
constellations_other.description: Early sound equipment allowed filmmakers and amateurs alike the ability to experiment often with affordable 16mm film.

end.creator_credit_for_label: Kevin Williamson, Paul Stupin, Tom Kapinos, Columbia TriStar Television, Granville Productions, Outerbanks Entertainment, Procter & Gamble Productions (PGP) and Sony Pictures Television Studios

links.description: The opening credits of Dawson’s Creek were also shot on 16mm. The TV debut for creator Kevin Williamson was the breakout role for Michelle Williams, who’s since often worked with Kelly Reichardt and other indie filmmakers.
